Georeferencing Report

65b Borrhålskarta med dagskarta.jpg — Sheet 65B

Executive Summary

POOR   The georeferencing of this map sheet is rated poor.

29 ground control points were identified across an 8×4 grid on the scanned image. 3 grid positions could not be matched, typically where map content (borehole annotations, labels, or linework) obscured the printed crosses. No outliers were detected.

The root-mean-square positional error across all control points is 34.35 m, well within the expected tolerance for a scanned map of this type. The georeferenced output is in SWEREF99 TM (EPSG:3006) and is ready for use in GIS.

Control Point Overlay

The image below shows each detected control point plotted on the scanned map. Green markers indicate accepted points; red markers indicate statistical outliers.

Each control point was identified by template-matching a synthetic cross pattern against the scanned image. Positions are refined to sub-pixel accuracy using parabolic interpolation on the correlation surface.

Residual Analysis

A six-parameter affine transformation was fitted from pixel coordinates to ST74 map coordinates. The residuals below show how well each control point fits this model. Points exceeding twice the RMS (68.700 m) are flagged as outliers.

The affine model is used only for internal quality assessment, not for the final georeferencing. A low RMS indicates that the detected cross positions form a geometrically consistent grid.

Coordinate Transformation

Each control point was transformed from the source coordinate system (ST74, EPSG:3152) to the output system (SWEREF99 TM, EPSG:3006). Both systems use the GRS80 ellipsoid, so no datum shift is required. The transformation is equivalent to Lantmäteriet GTRANS.

proj=pipeline step inv proj=tmerc lat_0=0 lon_0=18.05779 k=0.99999425 x_0=100178.1808 y_0=-6500614.7836 ellps=GRS80 step proj=utm zone=33 ellps=GRS80

Step 1: Inverse ST74 Transverse Mercator → geographic on GRS80.
Step 2: Forward UTM zone 33 → SWEREF99 TM.
Round-trip accuracy: < 0.001 mm.

Processing Methodology

This section documents the complete processing chain so that the georeferencing can be independently reproduced or audited.

1. Sheet identification

The map sheet code (65B) was extracted from the filename. The code encodes the sheet’s position within the Stockholm borehole map grid: the first digit is the column (easting group), the second is the row (northing group), and the letter indicates the quadrant (a=NW, b=NE, c=SW, d=SE). Each sub-sheet covers 800 m × 500 m. Coordinate crosses are printed at 100 m intervals in both easting and northing.

2. Cross detection

A synthetic cross template (arm length 20 px, line thickness 3 px) was matched against the greyscale image using normalised cross-correlation (cv2.matchTemplate with TM_CCOEFF_NORMED). Peaks above a correlation threshold of 0.7 were extracted via non-maximum suppression (window size 41 px).

Each candidate was verified by analysing the pixel intensity profile: the horizontal and vertical arms must be dark (mean < 170), the centre must be dark (< 160), and the four diagonal corners must be significantly brighter than the arms (contrast > 25). This rejects false positives such as borehole symbols, text characters, and linework intersections.

Duplicate detections within 50 px were merged, keeping the highest-correlation candidate. Positions were refined to sub-pixel accuracy using parabolic interpolation on the correlation surface.

3. Grid organisation

Detected crosses were clustered into columns and rows using 1D gap-based clustering (minimum gap 500 px). The interior grid rows were identified by finding the longest chain of consistently spaced rows (tolerance 12%), then constraining to the expected count from the sheet geometry (4 interior rows for a 500 m sheet).

4. Affine-validated gap filling

A least-squares affine model was fitted from the directly detected crosses. For each empty grid cell, a local template search was performed around the affine-predicted position. A gap-filled point was accepted only if its correlation exceeded 0.65 and its position was within 15 px of the prediction. Points that failed either criterion were excluded, preventing incorrect matches in areas where map content obscures the cross.

5. Coordinate assignment

ST74 coordinates were assigned based on each point’s grid position and the sheet boundaries. The first detected column corresponds to the sheet’s minimum easting (99200), incrementing by 100 m per column. The first interior row corresponds to the sheet’s maximum northing minus 100 m (77900), decrementing by 100 m per row.

6. Transformation

Coordinates were transformed from ST74 (EPSG:3152) to SWEREF99 TM (EPSG:3006) using pyproj.Transformer. The PROJ pipeline inverts the ST74 Transverse Mercator projection, then applies the UTM zone 33 forward projection. Both systems share the GRS80 ellipsoid and SWEREF99/ETRS89 realisation, so no datum shift is required.

7. Output

The final VRT file contains the transformed GCPs in SWEREF99 TM and can be opened directly in QGIS or processed with GDAL tools.

Tools and libraries

  • OpenCV (cv2) — template matching, non-maximum suppression
  • NumPy — array operations, least-squares fitting
  • Pillow (PIL) — image loading
  • pyproj — coordinate transformation (PROJ wrapper)
  • Matplotlib — overlay plot generation
